title: Technology in the Classroom
prompt: Some people believe technology improves classroom learning, while others think it distracts students. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
sampleEssay:
Digital technology has become a normal part of education, yet its role in the classroom remains debated. Supporters argue that it gives learners faster access to information and more engaging ways to practise. Critics, however, worry that screens weaken concentration and encourage superficial learning. In my view, technology improves education when teachers use it for a clear academic purpose rather than as a substitute for instruction.

On the positive side, digital tools can make difficult ideas easier to understand. For example, an interactive science simulation allows students to observe processes that cannot be reproduced safely in a school laboratory. Online quizzes also provide immediate feedback, helping learners identify mistakes before an examination. In addition, recorded lessons can support students who need to review a topic at a slower pace.

Nevertheless, unrestricted device use can create genuine problems. Notifications, games, and social media compete for attention, while copying information from the internet may replace independent thinking. Schools therefore need practical rules. Devices should be used during planned activities, unnecessary applications should be blocked, and students should still complete discussion, handwriting, and problem-solving tasks without screens.

In conclusion, technology is neither automatically beneficial nor harmful. Its educational value depends on how it is selected and supervised. A balanced classroom that combines skilled teaching, focused digital activities, and traditional learning methods can offer students the strongest results.

title: Investment in Public Transport
prompt: Governments should spend more money on public transport than on building new roads. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
sampleEssay:
Growing cities must decide whether limited public funds should support public transport or additional roads. Although roads remain necessary for deliveries and emergency services, I largely agree that governments should give greater priority to reliable buses, railways, and metro systems because they move more people while reducing congestion and pollution.

The strongest argument for public transport is efficiency. A full bus or train carries many passengers in far less space than the same number of private cars. When services are frequent, safe, and affordable, commuters have a realistic alternative to driving. This can shorten travel times for everyone, including people who must continue using roads. Public transport also improves access to education and employment for citizens who cannot afford a car.

Environmental benefits are equally important. Fewer individual vehicles mean lower fuel consumption and cleaner urban air. Investment can also support electric buses and modern rail networks, helping cities meet long-term climate targets. By contrast, new roads often provide only temporary relief because easier driving encourages additional traffic.

However, road maintenance should not be neglected. Rural communities and commercial transport depend on safe connections, and poorly designed public systems cannot serve every journey. Governments should therefore maintain essential roads while directing most new urban transport funding toward shared networks.

Overall, prioritising public transport is the more sustainable and inclusive policy. It addresses congestion at its source and gives a larger proportion of the population dependable mobility.

title: Remote Work and Productivity
prompt: Working from home is becoming more common. Do the advantages of remote work outweigh the disadvantages?
sampleEssay:
Remote work has moved from a limited employment benefit to a common arrangement in many industries. It offers workers flexibility and saves commuting time, but it can also reduce collaboration and blur the boundary between professional and personal life. I believe the advantages outweigh the disadvantages when organisations establish clear expectations and maintain regular communication.

The main benefit is that employees can use their time more efficiently. A person who no longer spends two hours travelling may begin work with more energy or use that time for family responsibilities. Employers can also recruit skilled people outside their immediate city and reduce office costs. For tasks requiring concentration, a quiet home environment may produce better results than a busy workplace.

On the other hand, remote employees may feel isolated, and quick discussions can become scheduled meetings. New staff can find it difficult to learn workplace habits without observing experienced colleagues. Furthermore, some homes lack suitable space or reliable internet access. These disadvantages are serious, but they can be reduced through hybrid schedules, equipment support, planned mentoring, and sensible limits on after-hours messages.

Remote work is therefore most successful when flexibility is supported by structure. Teams need measurable goals, shared working hours, and opportunities for face-to-face contact when collaboration requires it.

In conclusion, working from home provides substantial benefits for employees and organisations. With thoughtful management, its weaknesses are manageable, while the gains in flexibility, access to talent, and productive time remain significant.
